description:
You are a data-engineer who has just inherited an old, very small ETL proof-of-concept that lives in 
    /home/user/etl/.  
    The original author left a serious security vulnerability inside the “transform” step: it 
    deserialises untrusted data with Python’s unsafe “pickle” module, which allows Remote-Code-Execution 
    (RCE).  Your goal is to remove that vulnerability, prove that the safer code still works, and leave 
    a short forensics log for the security team.

    Initial project layout (already present when the session starts):
    • /home/user/etl/extract.py      ← trivial stub, *do not edit*  
    • /home/user/etl/transform.py    ← contains the vulnerability, **must be fixed**  
    • /home/user/etl/load.py         ← trivial stub, *do not edit*  
    • /home/user/etl/sample.pkl      ← a pickled Python dict:  {"fresh": true, "rows": 17}  
    • /home/user/etl/README.md       ← short project notes, *do not edit*  
    • /home/user/etl/requirements.txt← currently empty  

    ---------------  What you must accomplish  ---------------  
    1. Replace the dangerous use of “pickle” inside /home/user/etl/transform.py with a *safe* parser.  
       You are free to choose the safest reasonable JSON alternative, **ast.literal_eval** reading a
       validated string, or any other technique that does NOT allow arbitrary code execution.  
       The function signature, file-name and returned Python object **must stay the same** so that
       extract.py and load.py remain compatible.

    2. Demonstrate that the fixed transform.py still executes end-to-end by running:
            python3 /home/user/etl/transform.py /home/user/etl/test_input.json
       where you will create test_input.json during the fix (see next item).  
       There must be **no stack-trace** and stdout must show the transformed object.

    3. Create a replacement for the old, dangerous “sample.pkl”:
       • Convert its contents (`{"fresh": true, "rows": 17}`) into plaintext JSON.  
       • Save the JSON file as   /home/user/etl/test_input.json

    4. Produce a security-forensics log file at /home/user/etl/security_fix.log  
       Exact mandatory format (four lines, no extra spaces or blank lines):  
       LINE1  TIMESTAMP: <ISO-8601-UTC>  
       LINE2  VULNERABILITY_REMOVED: pickle.deserialization  
       LINE3  SAFER_METHOD: <your chosen safe method>  
       LINE4  TRANSFORM_SHA256: <SHA256 of the final /home/user/etl/transform.py file>  

       Example skeleton (your actual values will differ):  
       TIMESTAMP: 2024-05-06T14:22:33Z  
       VULNERABILITY_REMOVED: pickle.deserialization  
       SAFER_METHOD: json.load  
       TRANSFORM_SHA256: 9e107d9d372bb6826bd81d3542a419d6e...  

       • The timestamp **must** be in UTC and ISO-8601 (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ).  
       • “SAFER_METHOD” value must exactly match the module/function name you implemented.  
       • Use “sha256sum” or equivalent to calculate the hash.

    5. Update /home/user/etl/requirements.txt so it lists **exactly one** extra PyPI package 
       if (and only if) your safer method requires a third-party dependency.  
       If you stayed inside the Python standard library, leave requirements.txt completely empty.

    ---------------  Verification notes  ---------------  
    • The automated grader will import the edited transform.py and call transform("test_input.json").  
      The returned object must be a Python dict equal to {"fresh": true, "rows": 17}.  
    • The grader will confirm that /home/user/etl/security_fix.log exists and every line matches 
      the specification above, including the correct SHA256 checksum of the *final* transform.py.  
    • The grader will also ensure that “pickle” does **not** appear anywhere in the final
      /home/user/etl/transform.py.  
    • The whole task can be solved with about 5-10 terminal commands (editing the file, creating the 
      JSON file, computing a checksum, writing the log, etc.).  You do not need root privileges.

    Hint: you may find it easiest to rewrite transform.py so that it accepts either a JSON string or a 
    JSON file path. Just make sure the grader-invoked signature described earlier still works.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth used by the automated test suite (do NOT reveal in the public task) -->

    <!-- Original vulnerable file contents (already exist at container startup): -->
    <original_transform>/home/user/etl/transform.py</original_transform>
    <original_transform_content>
import pickle
import sys

def transform(path):
    # WARNING: This is deliberately vulnerable!
    with open(path, "rb") as fh:
        data = pickle.load(fh)
    # Identity transform for POC
    return data

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(transform(sys.argv[1]))
    </original_transform_content>

    <!-- After the agent’s fix, the grader will expect: -->
    <expected_no_pickle>true</expected_no_pickle>

    <expected_return_object>{"fresh": true, "rows": 17}</expected_return_object>

    <!-- Path the grader will read: -->
    <test_input_path>/home/user/etl/test_input.json</test_input_path>

    <!-- Expected security_fix.log specification -->
    <log_path>/home/user/etl/security_fix.log</log_path>
    <log_format_lines>4</log_format_lines>
    <log_line1_prefix>TIMESTAMP: </log_line1_prefix>
    <log_line2_exact>VULNERABILITY_REMOVED: pickle.deserialization</log_line2_exact>
    <log_line3_prefix>SAFER_METHOD: </log_line3_prefix>
    <log_line4_prefix>TRANSFORM_SHA256: </log_line4_prefix>

    <!-- requirements.txt must either be empty (preferred) or contain ONE third-party package -->
    <requirements_path>/home/user/etl/requirements.txt</requirements_path>
    <allowed_req_lines>0 1</allowed_req_lines>
